content.text: Claude Cowork is Anthropic's agentic knowledge-work platform. Where the Claude chat interface handles individual questions and the Claude API lets developers build applications, Cowork positions Claude as a persistent, action-capable AI that operates directly inside your working environment — reading files, running code, browsing the web, calling connected apps, and completing multi-step tasks with or without a human present at each step.
The diagram below shows the six capability rings around the central agent engine, and the human-in-the-loop connection that runs through all of them.

attrs.alt: Claude Cowork platform architecture: agent engine surrounded by six capability rings with human-in-the-loop
attrs.title: Claude Cowork Platform Architecture

content.text: The core insight behind Cowork is that most knowledge work is not a single Q&A exchange — it is a sequence of actions spread across files, apps, and time. A task like "prepare the Q3 competitive brief" involves reading documents, searching the web, synthesising findings, drafting text, formatting a report, and sending it to colleagues. Cowork lets Claude execute that whole chain, not just answer questions about it.
Three properties define what makes Cowork different from conversational Claude:
: Claude takes actions in the world — writing files, executing code, calling connectors — rather than only producing text.
: Projects maintain memory across sessions. Standing instructions, shared documents, and preferences survive when you close the window and return tomorrow.
: Every consequential action is gated by a permission model. You decide in advance what Cowork may do autonomously and what requires an approval prompt.

At runtime, Cowork runs a plan-and-act loop. Given a task, Claude decomposes it into subtasks, selects the right tool for each, executes them in order (or in parallel via subagents), checks intermediate results, and adapts if something fails. This loop runs inside a managed environment that constrains which tools are available and logs every action for the human to review.

The loop is not prompt-chaining in the naïve sense. Claude reasons about its own plan: it can backtrack, retry a failed step with a different approach, or surface a decision point to the user rather than guessing. The agent reads its task output after each step and decides whether it is correct before proceeding.

In chat you write a prompt and Claude writes a response. In Cowork you describe a goal and Claude figures out the steps, executes them, and hands you the result — interrupting only when a decision genuinely requires you.

Cowork ships as a native desktop application for macOS and Windows. The desktop app gives Claude access to your local file system — it can read source files, write drafts, move documents, and run terminal commands within scoped directories. Browser control lets Claude open URLs, extract page content, fill forms, and download assets.

File access is not open-ended. You configure which directories Cowork may read and which it may write. Actions outside the permitted scope trigger an approval prompt or are blocked entirely, depending on your settings.

Cowork can search the web, fetch URLs, and browse pages to gather live information. Code execution runs in a sandboxed environment — Claude can run Python, shell scripts, or Node.js, read their output, and iterate on the result. The sandbox is isolated from your system; code cannot reach file paths or network endpoints outside the explicit grant you configure.

Connectors extend Cowork into third-party services. OAuth-based connectors let Claude read and write Google Drive documents, post Slack messages, open GitHub issues, update Jira tickets, and send or search email. The Chrome Extension connector gives Cowork access to the page currently open in your browser, enabling workflows that combine a live web page with local files and connected services.

Each connector has a declared capability scope (read-only vs read-write, specific resource types). You grant access per-connector during setup; Cowork will not use a connector you have not authorised.
A Cowork Project is a named workspace that groups related tasks with a shared context. Each project carries:
: A system-level prompt that shapes how Claude behaves in this project — preferred tone, domain-specific rules, output format requirements.
: Files added to the project are available to every task in it without re-uploading.
: Prior task threads are retained so Claude can reference decisions made in earlier sessions.
: The connectors authorised for this project are set once and reused across all tasks within it.

A practical example: a "Product Launch" project might carry the brand guide as a shared document, a standing instruction that all copy must be under 200 words, and a Google Drive connector binding. Every task in that project inherits all three automatically.
Some tasks cannot finish in a single request-response cycle. Cowork supports two patterns for extending beyond a single context window:
: A task configured to run on a cron-like schedule. Cowork runs it in the background at the specified time and notifies you when it completes or encounters a decision point.

: The orchestrator Claude can spawn specialised child agents to work in parallel on independent subtasks — for example, one subagent researches competitor A while another researches competitor B, and the orchestrator synthesises both results.

Subagents inherit a subset of the parent's permissions; they cannot exceed the access level the project has been granted. Scheduled tasks run under the same permission model as interactive tasks — the schedule does not bypass any approval gates.
Cowork's permission model is layered. Actions are classified by consequence, and each class has a default disposition that admins and users can adjust:

Approval prompts are surfaced inline during the task. You can approve, deny, or modify the proposed action before Claude proceeds. If you approve an action class for the remainder of a session, Cowork will not ask again until the session ends.

Sandbox environments isolate the code-execution surface from the rest of your machine. Code that Claude runs cannot reach file paths or network endpoints outside the configured grant — not just by policy, but by OS-level isolation.
Cowork vs Claude Chat vs Claude Code
The three Claude surfaces serve different audiences and task shapes:
Cowork has an admin tier designed for organizations deploying it to a team. Admins can:
Create shared projects that all team members can access and contribute to.
Set organization-wide connector policies — for example, requiring that Slack write access always requires an approval prompt, regardless of user preference.
View usage logs: which tasks ran, which actions were approved, and which were blocked.
Publish curated plugins that appear in the team's Cowork interface, pre-configured with the right connector bindings.

Admin policies layer on top of user settings — a user cannot relax a restriction that the admin has locked, but can tighten a setting further (for example, requiring their own additional approval on an action the admin allows automatically).

Plugins package a specific workflow into a reusable unit. A plugin bundles a prompt template, a set of connectors, file scope settings, and an optional skill definition into a single installable component. Installing a plugin adds a named capability to your Cowork interface — for example, a "Weekly Status Report" plugin that reads the week's GitHub activity, summarises it, and drafts a Slack post.

Individual users can install plugins from the marketplace. Admins can publish internal plugins to their team's private registry, pre-approving the connector grants so individual users do not need to configure them.
A few boundaries worth stating explicitly, because they affect how you design workflows:
: Cowork executes tasks sequentially (or in parallel via subagents), but it is not an event-driven daemon. Triggers are scheduled or user-initiated, not reactive to incoming webhooks in general.

: Cowork is optimised for tasks that require language understanding and reasoning. Pure data-pipeline or infrastructure automation is better handled by dedicated tools (GitHub Actions, Airflow, Zapier) and can then be called
Cowork via a connector or shell command.
: The default permission model requires human approval for consequential actions. Full autonomy is an opt-in that requires explicit admin and user configuration.
To go from installation to a working first task:
for your OS and sign in with your Anthropic account.
. Give it a name and write a brief standing instruction (e.g. "This project is for drafting internal engineering blog posts. Use a direct technical tone. Do not include marketing language.").
. Add the directories Cowork may read from and the directories it may write to. Start narrow — expand as you understand what the agent needs.
. Pick the connector most relevant to your first task (Google Drive for document work, GitHub for code review, etc.) and complete the OAuth flow.
. Describe the goal in plain language. Watch the approval prompts as they appear — they show exactly what the agent is about to do and give you a chance to redirect before it acts.

Walking through the lifecycle of a single Cowork task makes the pieces concrete. Suppose you type: "Draft a competitive analysis of our three main rivals based on their latest blog posts and pricing pages. Save it to the reports folder as competitive-q3.md."

: Claude reads the project's standing instructions and context, then creates an internal plan: identify the three rivals from project memory or ask if unknown; for each rival, search the web for their blog and pricing page; synthesise; format as Markdown.
: The plan maps to tools: web search for blog discovery, URL fetch for pricing pages, no connector needed (rivals are public), file write for the output.
: Three subagents are spawned, one per rival, each fetching and summarising independently. The orchestrator waits for all three.
to the reports folder, Cowork surfaces an approval prompt showing the full file path and a preview of the first 200 characters. You approve.
: The file is written. Cowork reports the result: "Draft saved to reports/competitive-q3.md — 1,240 words across 3 sections." The task thread is stored in the project for future reference.

The entire sequence — web search, subagent spawning, synthesis, write — ran without you switching tabs. The one decision you made (approve the file write) was the only point where Cowork needed you. That ratio — many automated steps, one human decision — is the Cowork design intent.
Choosing the Right Surface: Cowork, Chat, or Code
Picking the wrong surface wastes time. Here are the decision signals that indicate Cowork is the right choice:
The task requires reading from or writing to more than one file or service. If you would otherwise copy-paste between apps, Cowork handles that.
The task has sub-steps whose order depends on the result of earlier steps. Cowork's plan-and-act loop handles conditional branching; chat cannot.
The task recurs on a schedule (weekly report, daily summary). Use a Cowork scheduled task rather than remembering to prompt chat each time.
The output needs to be saved, versioned, or shared. Cowork writes to files or connected apps; chat outputs exist only in the conversation.

Stay on chat when you need a quick answer, a single draft, or exploratory brainstorming — the overhead of configuring a project and permissions is not worth it for a ten-second question. Use Claude Code when the task is entirely within a code repository and the engineer wants fine-grained control over every file edit.
Most Cowork tasks map to one of four patterns. Recognising the pattern early helps you write better task descriptions and configure the right connectors.

Claude searches the web (or reads connected documents), extracts relevant information, and produces a structured output — a report, briefing, FAQ, or summary. Subagents work in parallel on independent sources; the orchestrator synthesises. Typical connectors: Google Drive (for reference docs), web search, file write.

Claude drafts a document — blog post, email campaign, proposal, spec — reading existing materials from the project, writing the draft to a file, and (optionally) sending it to a reviewer via a Slack or email connector. You iterate by commenting in the task thread; Claude updates the file in place. Typical connectors: Google Drive, email, Slack.

Claude reads structured or semi-structured input (CSV, JSON, a web table, a PDF), extracts fields according to a schema you describe, and writes the result in a different format. The sandboxed code runner handles large-volume transformation with Python when a simple prompt is not enough. Typical connectors: local files, web fetch, code execution.

A scheduled Cowork task polls a data source (a website, a connected app, a file) on a cron interval, compares the current state to a baseline stored in the project, and sends a Slack or email notification only when a meaningful change is detected. Typical connectors: web fetch, Slack, email, Google Sheets (for baseline).
Cowork tasks do not always succeed on the first attempt. The agent handles failures in three ways:
: If a web fetch returns an error or a connector call fails transiently, Claude retries with exponential backoff before escalating.

: If one data source is unavailable, Claude can substitute an alternative — for example, using a cached file instead of a live connector call, or using a different search query that returns comparable results.

: If the failure cannot be resolved autonomously — a required connector is not authorised, a file does not exist, a required input is ambiguous — Claude pauses the task and asks you directly. The task thread shows exactly what was attempted and what is missing.

Partial results are preserved. If a task that processes ten documents fails on document seven, the outputs for documents one through six are already written. When you resolve the issue and resume, Claude continues from where it stopped rather than restarting from scratch.

Cowork includes a role-picker onboarding flow that installs a curated set of skills and plugins tuned to your job function. A product manager gets document-drafting, competitor-tracking, and sprint-planning plugins pre-configured. An engineer gets code-review, documentation, and incident-log plugins. A marketer gets content-research, SEO-analysis, and campaign-drafting plugins.

Under the hood, a skill is a reusable agent specification — a system prompt, a tool allowlist, and a set of connector bindings — that can be invoked by name from any task in Cowork. Skills reduce repetitive configuration: instead of setting up the same connectors and instructions every time you run a competitor analysis, you invoke the "Competitor Analysis" skill and it applies the standard setup automatically.

You can author custom skills in Markdown with a YAML frontmatter block that declares the skill's name, tools, and instructions. Team admins publish skills to the shared registry so every team member can invoke them consistently. The marketplace surfaces community-published skills alongside the Anthropic-curated set.
Cowork processes data in the context of Anthropic's standard API usage policies. A few specifics matter for enterprise deployment:
Connector data stays within the session

: When Claude reads a Google Drive document to complete a task, that document's content is used to produce the task output and is not stored by Anthropic for model training purposes (subject to the applicable usage terms).
: The task thread — what Claude planned, what actions it took, what approvals were given — is stored in the project for audit and reference. Admins can configure retention periods.

: Connector OAuth tokens are stored encrypted and are never included in Claude's context window. Claude calls connectors via a managed proxy that injects the token at the infrastructure layer, not at the prompt layer.
Measuring Success: What Good Cowork Usage Looks Like

A reliable signal that you are using Cowork well: you feel slightly bored during a task run. That is the agent doing the mechanical work — searching, reading, formatting — while you wait to review the result. If you find yourself intervening on every approval prompt or constantly redirecting the task, the task description is probably too ambiguous or the standing instructions need refinement.
Practical benchmarks for a well-configured Cowork setup:
A task that previously took you 90 minutes of manual work completes in under 10 minutes of agent time, with 2–3 approval prompts that each take under 30 seconds to review.
Recurring tasks (weekly reports, daily digests) run unattended and land in the right place without you remembering to trigger them.
New team members can pick up a project and understand what Claude is doing and why, because the standing instructions and task log are legible documentation.

Cowork degrades gracefully when it fails. A partial output with a clear explanation of what is missing is more useful than a hallucinated complete output. The oversight model is designed to produce the former — approval gates and explicit decision points prevent Claude from filling gaps silently.
Limitations to Know Before You Start
Understanding where Cowork falls short saves debugging time:
Context window limits apply to subagents too

: Each subagent has its own context window. If a subagent is asked to process a 500-page document, it will lose early content as it reads through the file. Chunk large documents and pass summaries between stages rather than the raw content.

: OAuth tokens for connected apps expire and must be refreshed. A scheduled task that runs at 3 AM against an expired token will fail and surface a decision point asking you to re-authenticate. Set a calendar reminder to check connector auth health monthly.

: When Claude browses web pages, it reads what is publicly accessible — which includes paywalled stubs, JavaScript-rendered content that the headless browser may not load fully, and pages that have changed since the URL was indexed. Treat web-sourced content as a starting point to verify, not a ground truth.

: Cowork limits concurrent subagents per task to prevent runaway resource use. If your task fans out to more workers than the cap allows, the excess are queued and execute in batches. The cap is configured at the admin level and visible in the team's governance settings.

Claude Cowork is an agentic platform that closes the gap between "Claude can help with this" and "Claude can do this". The agent engine plans and executes multi-step knowledge workflows; Projects give it persistent context and standing instructions; Connectors let it reach the apps where work lives; the permission and oversight layer keeps a human in control of consequential actions. The topics that follow cover each capability in depth — starting with how the agent engine works, then the connector model, permissions, long-running tasks, and team administration.
